title: Understanding Text Embeddings with Hugging Face

source.submodule: Using Groq API for AI Tasks

topicTags: Using Groq API for AI Tasks

content.problemStatement:
So far, you have learned how to send text to an LLM and receive generated responses. However, generation is only one part of what modern AI systems can do. In many real-world applications, we want machines to **understand and compare text**, not just produce new text.

This is where **text embeddings** become important.

Embeddings convert text into numeric vectors that capture semantic meaning. Texts with similar meanings result in vectors that are close to each other, even if the wording is different.

For example:
- **Deep learning is powerful**
- **Neural networks can solve complex problems**

These two sentences would have similar embeddings because their meanings are related.

---

**What We Are Using**
We will use a **sentence-transformer model** from Hugging Face:
- Model: `sentence-transformers/all-MiniLM-L6-v2`

This model is designed to convert sentences into meaningful numeric vectors and works well for similarity and comparison tasks.

We access it through the **Hugging Face Inference API**, which means:
- No training required
- No heavy setup
- Just send text and receive embeddings

---

**Setup Requirements**
Before running the code:
- Install the required library: ```pip install huggingface-hub```


- Get a Hugging Face API token:
- Visit https://huggingface.co/join and sign up or log in
- Go to Profile → Settings → Access Tokens. For this **demo**, select the role "Read".
- Generate a new token and copy it

This token allows your Python program to securely use Hugging Face models.

---

**High-Level Flow**
Here is what happens in the demonstration:

- We initialize a Hugging Face client using your API token
- We prepare a list of short text sentences
- We send these texts to the embedding model
- The model returns numeric vectors (embeddings)
- Each vector represents the meaning of one sentence

In short, **embeddings** are a smarter way of converting **text** into **numbers**, just like we did earlier with TF-IDF or token IDs, but here done automatically by a pretrained model.
These numeric vectors can then be compared, stored, or fed directly into LLMs, exactly how modern AI systems work in real life.

---

**Expected Output**
```code
Embeddings generated successfully!
Shape of embeddings: (3, 384)

First embedding vector (truncated):
[-0.03146001  0.0176859   0.05343606 -0.00757904  0.0620981   0.00035088
  0.01344709 -0.09568137 -0.03949122 -0.03243538]
```
constraints:
- $1 \leq T \leq 100$
- $1 \leq N \leq 100$
- $1 \leq M \leq N\cdot(N-1)/2$
- $1 \leq u_i, v_i \leq N$
- $u_i \neq v_i$ for each $1 \leq i \leq M$.
- The sum of $N$ over all test cases won't exceed $100$.
content.inputFormat:
- The first line of input will contain a single integer $T$, denoting the number of test cases.
- Each test case consists of multiple lines of input.
    - The first line of each test case contains two space-separated integers $N$ and $M$ — the number of vertices and edges of the graph, respectively.
    - The next $M$ lines describe the edges. The $i$-th of these $M$ lines contains two space-separated integers $u_i$ and $v_i$, denoting an edge between $u_i$ and $v_i$.
content.outputFormat: For each test case, output on a new line the number of good subgraphs of $G$, modulo $10^9 + 7$.
